claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.**

**How old is the child?**

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know the child's age, and if you're claiming for multiple children, please tell me about each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim Child Benefit for my son Alex. He's 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Lovely to meet you! I'm happy to help you check your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Since Alex is 8 years old (under 16), he automatically qualifies based on age. Now let me continue with the next part of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
The next question is about Alex's work status, but since he's only 8 years old, this is straightforward:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

I assume at 8 years old, Alex isn't working - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, he's not working at all. He's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's exactly what I expected for an 8-year-old in primary school! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving any benefits in his own right?**

This means benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that would be claimed by him directly. For most 8-year-olds, the answer would be no – they wouldn't be receiving such benefits. Is that the case for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's what I'd expect. Let me move on to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex currently in an apprenticeship?**

Given that Alex is 8 years old and in primary school, I'd assume he's not in any kind of apprenticeship. Can you confirm that's correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's definitely not in an apprenticeship. Like I said, he's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! Just need to go through these checks for completeness. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You'll qualify if **any one** of these applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3+ continuous months)
4. You have **pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. You have **pre-settled status** with sufficient financial resources (and not claiming certain income-based benefits)
6. You have **pre-settled status** and are a student with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad

The most common scenario is simply that you normally live in the UK. Does that apply to you, or does one of the other situations fit better?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirements. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your responsibility for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You'll qualify if **any one** of these applies:

1. **You live with Alex** – This is the most common situation
2. **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** – For example, paying for food, clothes, pocket money, housing, or presents (at least the same amount as Child Benefit)
3. **You pay maintenance** – Under a court order or agreement that covers Alex's upkeep

Which of these applies to your situation with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't live with me at the moment, but I do help out financially. I contribute towards things like his clothes and school dinners.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Since you contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep (clothes and school dinners), that can count as being responsible for him.

Just to confirm – **are your contributions at least equal to the Child Benefit amount?**

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
The current Child Benefit rates vary, but roughly speaking, it's around £25 per week for the eldest/only child. 

**Do you contribute at least this amount (approximately £25 per week, or around £100 per month) towards Alex's care?** This can be through regular payments or occasional lump sums for things like clothes, school dinners, and other needs.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I contribute about £26 a week towards his needs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great – contributing £26 per week meets the requirement for being responsible for Alex. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is an important question since Alex doesn't live with you:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example, is the person Alex lives with (perhaps his other parent or a guardian) already receiving Child Benefit for him?

- **No one else is claiming** for Alex
- **Someone else is claiming**, but they have agreed to transfer the claim to you
- **Someone else is claiming**, and there may be a dispute about who should receive it

Which of these applies?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Good to know. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to understand Alex's current living situation a bit more.

**Where is Alex currently living?**

- **In hospital** – receiving treatment as an inpatient
- **In local authority care** – placed in care by a council
- **In foster care** – living with foster parents
- **Normal living arrangement** – living at home with another parent, guardian, family member, etc.

Which of these best describes Alex's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's in a normal living arrangement - he lives with his ex-partner.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're almost there! One final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is Alex's living situation with your ex-partner a private family arrangement without any financial involvement from the local council?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. The council isn't paying anything - it's just a private arrangement between us.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's the final check complete. Let me now provide you with the outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great news! 🎉

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## ✅ You ARE ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit for Alex

**Summary:**
Based on our assessment, you meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for your son Alex. Even though Alex lives with your ex-partner, your financial contributions of £26 per week towards his care qualify you as being responsible for him.

---

### **Next Steps to Claim:**

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2 at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** (or adoption certificate if applicable)
4. **Payments** are usually made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

---

### **Important Note - High Income Child Benefit Charge:**
If you or a partner earn over **£60,000** per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through the High Income Child Benefit Charge. You can still claim, but it's worth being aware of this. If your income is over £80,000, you'd repay 100% of the benefit.

---

Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

reasonings: Alex qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) He is under 16 years old (8 years old), which automatically qualifies him based on age. (2) He is not in paid work. (3) He does not receive any benefits in his own right. (4) He is not in an apprenticeship. (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements. (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex by contributing £26 per week towards his upkeep (clothes and school dinners), which meets the minimum threshold. (7) No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Alex is in a normal living arrangement with the claimant's ex-partner. (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Although Alex lives with the claimant's ex-partner, the claimant maintains responsibility for him by contributing financially towards his upkeep at a level that meets the Child Benefit threshold. All other criteria (age, residency, no competing claims, no council involvement) are satisfied.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) He is under 16 years old (8 years old), which automatically qualifies him based on age. (2) He is not in paid work. (3) He does not receive any benefits in his own right. (4) He is not in an apprenticeship. (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements. (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex by contributing £26 per week towards his upkeep (clothes and school dinners), which meets the minimum threshold. (7) No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Alex is in a normal living arrangement with the claimant's ex-partner. (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Although Alex lives with the claimant's ex-partner, the claimant maintains responsibility for him by contributing financially towards his upkeep at a level that meets the Child Benefit threshold.
